text:
CT NECK WITH CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with biopsy-proven squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, p16-positive. Evaluation for extent of locoregional disease prior to multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and treatment planning.

TECHNIQUE: Axial CT images of the neck were obtained from the skull base through the thoracic inlet following intravenous administration of iohexol contrast (90 mL, injected at 3 mL/sec). Arterial and venous phase acquisitions performed. Multiplanar reformats in coronal and sagittal planes reviewed. Slice thickness 1.25 mm reconstructed to 2.5 mm for review. No prior neck CT available for comparison.

FINDINGS:

Primary tumor:
There is a heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue mass centered in the right base of tongue measuring approximately 3.1 cm in its greatest dimension. The mass is predominantly submucosal with a lobulated contour and demonstrates avid but heterogeneous contrast enhancement suggesting internal necrosis or cystic change. It involves the right glossoepiglottic fold. The lesion does not appear to cross the midline. No extension into the vallecula is identified. The epiglottis is normal in appearance and the pre-epiglottic fat space is preserved without infiltration. The pterygoid musculature, masticator space, and parapharyngeal fat planes appear clear bilaterally. There is no radiographically apparent involvement of the mandible or adjacent bony structures. The hard and soft palate are unremarkable. The oral tongue proper appears uninvolved.

Lymph nodes:
A right-sided level IIA cervical lymph node measures 2.4 x 1.8 cm in axial dimension. This node demonstrates internal heterogeneity with areas of low attenuation centrally raising concern for internal necrosis, highly suspicious for nodal metastatic disease. The fat planes surrounding this node are maintained without definitive evidence of extranodal extension on CT. No additional pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the right neck. The left cervical chain is unremarkable; no enlarged nodes meeting criteria for concern on the contralateral side. No bilateral level Ib, III, IV, V, or retropharyngeal adenopathy identified.

Airway:
The airway is patent throughout. The supraglottis, glottis, and subglottis are within normal limits. The true and false vocal cords are symmetric. Subglottic and tracheal lumen are unobstructed.

Thyroid gland:
The thyroid gland is mildly heterogeneous in echotexture, likely reflecting underlying thyroid disease in this patient with known hypothyroidism. No dominant nodule or suspicious lesion identified. No thyroid involvement by tumor.

Vascular structures:
The bilateral common and internal carotid arteries and jugular veins are patent. No vascular encasement by tumor.

Bone:
The visualized cervical spine and skull base demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions. No mandibular involvement.

Soft tissues:
No deep space neck infection or abscess. No subcutaneous or muscular abnormality apart from the primary lesion described above.

Parotid and submandibular glands:
Bilateral parotid and submandibular glands are symmetric and unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Heterogeneously enhancing right base of tongue mass measuring 3.1 cm with involvement of the right glossoepiglottic fold. No extension into the vallecula, epiglottis, or pre-epiglottic space. No extension across the midline. No involvement of adjacent bony structures or parapharyngeal space. Findings are consistent with the known biopsy-proven squamous cell carcinoma.

2. Enlarged right level IIA cervical lymph node (2.4 x 1.8 cm) with internal low attenuation consistent with necrotic nodal metastasis. No CT features to suggest definitive extranodal extension. No contralateral adenopathy.

3. Airway patent. No evidence of distant disease on the neck CT field of view.

4. Overall imaging findings are consistent with locoregional disease confined to the oropharynx and ipsilateral neck. Dedicated staging with PET/CT and/or MRI for treatment planning recommended as clinically appropriate.

Finding communicated to referring clinician at time of read.

Interpreting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Head and Neck Imaging]
Resident: [Radiology Resident]
Dictated and electronically signed.
